See three moves ahead: a row you fail to: See three moves ahead: a row you fail to complete is a row your rival completes.
Nine seeds each, three nested squares. W: Nine seeds each, three nested squares. Whoever lines up three in a connected row breaks one of the other's seeds. Plan the row before you place the seed.
The old players called it a game of fore: The old players called it a game of foresight — you do not win by the move you make, but by the two moves you have already seen.
Three in a line — a mill. Alignment is t: Three in a line — a mill. Alignment is the whole game: what you line up, you get to break.
A row completed. Notice you built it fro: A row completed. Notice you built it from an intersection that fed two lines at once — that is foresight, not luck.
The mill forms. A good player opens two: The mill forms. A good player opens two threats so the block of one completes the other.
You take an enemy seed — but never one a: You take an enemy seed — but never one already inside a mill, unless every enemy seed is. Choose the seed that would have hurt you most.
One seed removed. Take the piece that gu: One seed removed. Take the piece that guards a junction; cripple the network, not just the number.
The rival is down to two seeds and can f: The rival is down to two seeds and can form no line. Foresight, patiently applied, has won the board.
Reduced to two, you can no longer make a: Reduced to two, you can no longer make a row. The player who saw further took the board.
